items.q: How do stakeholder and shareholder interests overlap and conflict?
items.correct: Shareholders are a subset of stakeholders. Their interests overlap (long-term success benefits all) but conflict on issues like dividends vs investment, wages vs profits, or short-term gains vs long-term sustainability.
items.misconception: Stakeholders and shareholders always want the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Shareholders are external and stakeholders are internal.
items.why: The shareholder-stakeholder distinction is central to debates on corporate purpose. Students miss the structural tension — a board can't always please everyone simultaneously, which is what stakeholder analysis exists to examine.

items.q: How is Return on Capital Employed (ROCE) calculated?
items.correct: Operating profit divided by capital employed (total equity + non-current liabilities), expressed as a percentage. It measures how efficiently the business uses its long-term financing to generate profit.
items.misconception: ROCE is just net profit divided by revenue.
items.random_wrong: ROCE is the wages divided by hours worked.
items.why: ROCE compares profit to capital used, not revenue. It distinguishes capital-efficient businesses from revenue-heavy but capital-intensive ones. Students often confuse it with profit margin.

items.q: What do McGregor's Theory X and Theory Y describe?
items.correct: Two contrasting management assumptions: Theory X assumes workers dislike work and need control; Theory Y assumes workers can be self-motivated and seek responsibility.
items.misconception: Theory X is about working in a factory and Theory Y is about office work.
items.random_wrong: Theory X and Theory Y are stages of business growth.
items.why: The theories are about MANAGER assumptions, not industry or growth stages. McGregor's point is that managers' beliefs about workers shape how they manage and the outcomes they get.
Correct answer: Opportunities. “Outcome” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A new rival opening nearby is a…
items.why: It's Threat. “Strength” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A SWOT has 4 boxes. If 3 are filled, % complete?
items.why: The real figure is 75. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
